summary:
The Sistine Chapel Ceiling (1508–1512 AD) is Michelangelo's monumental fresco commission — one of the supreme achievements of Western art, painted over four years on the vault of the Vatican's private papal chapel at the commission of Pope Julius II. Michelangelo, primarily a sculptor who famously protested his lack of skill as a fresco painter, nonetheless created a unified theological program depicting nine scenes from Genesis (including the Creation of Adam, with its iconic reaching fingers), surrounded by prophets and sibyls, and hundreds of supporting figures in architectural frames — all executed in a scale, physical dynamism, and psychological complexity unprecedented in Western painting. Working largely alone (with assistants for preparatory tasks), lying on scaffolding for years, Michelangelo completed the ceiling in 1512.

The Sistine ceiling's influence on subsequent art was immediate and overwhelming — virtually every major painter of the 16th century felt compelled to respond to it, and its muscular, dynamic figures defined what was called the 'grand manner' (maniera grande) that dominated painting for a century. The artist would return to the Sistine Chapel nearly 25 years later to paint the Last Judgment (1536–1541) on the altar wall — a somber, terrifying vision of divine judgment quite different in tone from the ceiling's creative optimism, reflecting both Michelangelo's aging and the changed religious atmosphere following the Protestant Reformation. The Sistine Chapel remains the site of papal conclaves, where cardinals elect new popes — white smoke from its chimney signaling a papal election, a tradition unchanged since the Renaissance.
